processes.name: Judicial review
processes.plainName: Asking a court to check the decision was made lawfully
processes.oneLine: A court checks whether the decision was made the right way — not whether it was a good decision.
processes.question: Was the decision made lawfully?
processes.whatItIs: Judicial review is different from merits review. A court does not ask whether the decision was right or fair — it asks whether it was made lawfully: did the decision-maker have the power, follow a fair process, and stay within the law? If the court finds a legal error — a 'ground of review' — it can set the decision aside. But the court cannot make a new decision for you. It usually sends the matter back to be decided again, properly.

bodies.name: Supreme Court of Victoria (Order 56)
bodies.note: Judicial review of Victorian government decisions.

bodies.name: Federal Court / Federal Circuit and Family Court
bodies.note: Judicial review of Australian Government decisions (ADJR Act; s 39B Judiciary Act).

bodies.name: High Court (s 75(v) Constitution)
bodies.note: Constitutional judicial review of Commonwealth officers.

canApply: You need a 'ground of review' — a specific legal error in how the decision was made.
You usually need to be a person affected by the decision (standing).
There are strict time limits, and they are usually NOT paused by asking for reasons.
whatHappens: You start a proceeding in the court.
You argue that one or more grounds of review apply.
The court looks at the legality of the decision, not its merits.
If a ground is made out, the court can set the decision aside.
remedies: Send it back (remit) to be decided again, lawfully.
Orders to require or stop action, in some cases.
limits: The court cannot substitute its own decision — it cannot simply give you the outcome you want, only require a lawful re-decision.
Judicial review is about legality, not about whether the decision was harsh or wrong on the facts.
goodToKnow: Judicial review can be technical and the time limits are tight — getting free legal help early really matters.

processes.name: Merits review
processes.plainName: Asking for a fresh look at the decision
processes.oneLine: A tribunal looks at your case again and can make a different decision.
processes.question: Is this the correct or preferable decision?
processes.whatItIs: Merits review means an independent tribunal looks at the decision again — on the facts and the law — as if it were being made fresh. The tribunal stands in the shoes of the original decision-maker and applies the same rules. It can agree with the decision, change part of it, or replace it with a different one. It is usually cheaper, faster, and less formal than going to court.

bodies.name: VCAT (Victorian Civil and Administrative Tribunal)
bodies.note: Hears many Victorian decisions where an Act gives a right of review.

bodies.name: ART (Administrative Review Tribunal)
bodies.note: Hears many Australian Government decisions, such as Centrelink.

canApply: There must be a law that gives a right of review for this kind of decision.
You usually need to be a person affected by the decision (this is called standing).
There is almost always a time limit — this is the part people miss most.
whatHappens: You lodge an application with the tribunal.
Often there is an informal stage that tries to sort it out first.
If it goes to a hearing, you explain your side and give your evidence.
The tribunal makes a fresh decision.
remedies: Affirm — keep the decision the same.
Vary — change part of the decision.
Set aside and substitute — replace it with a new decision.
limits: Merits review is only available where a law provides it — not every decision can be reviewed on the merits.
goodToKnow: Because the tribunal can change the outcome, merits review is usually what people want when they disagree with the result.

comparison.intro: Most decisions can be challenged in one of two ways. They ask different questions, so the right one depends on what you are hoping for.

rows.mr: Is this the correct or preferable decision?

rows.jr: A court (Supreme Court or Federal Court)

rows.mr: The facts and the law, fresh
rows.jr: Only how the decision was made — its legality
Set it aside and send it back — not replace it
More formal, and often needs a lawyer

rows.mr: Only where a law gives a review right
rows.jr: Where there is a legal error (a ground of review)

options.prompt: A different outcome — I think the decision is wrong
options.because: If you want the decision changed, merits review (where it is available) is usually the path, because a tribunal can replace the decision.

options.prompt: The decision was made unfairly or without power
options.because: If the problem is how the decision was made, judicial review asks a court to check that it was lawful.

options.prompt: Both might apply, or I am not sure
options.because: Sometimes both are possible, and the time limits are different. A free legal service can help you choose — and quickly, because of the time limits.

grounds.name: Bad faith
grounds.plainName: The decision was not made honestly
grounds.oneLine: The decision was made dishonestly, or for a deliberately improper reason.
grounds.whatItMeans: A decision made dishonestly, or for a deliberately improper reason, can be unlawful. Bad faith is rare and hard to prove, and it is a serious allegation — it means much more than a mistake or even an unfair process.
grounds.plainExample: A decision is made to deliberately harm someone for personal reasons.
whatRelates: There are real signs the decision was not made honestly.
The decision looks deliberately aimed at harming you for an improper reason.
grounds.whatItIsNot: It does not cover an honest mistake or an unfair process — those are other grounds. Bad faith is a high bar and a serious claim.
grounds.test: A decision made in bad faith may be invalid; it is a serious allegation that needs strong material — VERIFY the exact test + authorities.

elements.name: Dishonesty or deliberate impropriety
elements.layPrompt: Are there real signs the decision was not made honestly?

grounds.name: Error of law
grounds.oneLine: The decision-maker applied the wrong legal test, or misread what the law requires.
grounds.whatItMeans: A decision can be challenged where the decision-maker made a legal error — for example, applying the wrong test, misreading what a law requires, or misunderstanding a legal word. Some legal errors are reviewable even when they appear on the face of the written decision.
grounds.plainExample: A decision applies a stricter test than the law actually sets.
whatRelates: The decision seems to apply the wrong rule or test.
The written reasons misstate what the law requires.
A legal word or requirement looks like it was misunderstood.
grounds.whatItIsNot: It does not cover a disagreement about the facts — it is about the law being applied wrongly.
grounds.test: An error of law (including one on the face of the record) may be reviewable — VERIFY the exact test + authorities.

elements.name: Wrong legal test
elements.layPrompt: Did they use the wrong legal test or standard?

elements.name: Misreading the law
elements.layPrompt: Do the reasons misstate what the law requires?

grounds.name: Improper purpose
grounds.plainName: The power was used for the wrong reason
grounds.oneLine: A power was used to achieve something other than what it was given for.
grounds.whatItMeans: A statutory power must be used for the purpose Parliament gave it. Using it to achieve something else — even something the decision-maker thinks is a good idea — can be unlawful.
grounds.plainExample: A permit is refused to punish someone for an unrelated complaint, rather than on the permit's merits.
whatRelates: The real reason for the decision seems to be something other than what the power is for.
The decision looks like it was made to achieve an unrelated aim.
grounds.whatItIsNot: It does not require a bad character — even a well-meant decision can be made for an improper purpose.
grounds.test: A statutory power must be used for the purpose for which it was given; using it for another purpose may be unlawful — VERIFY the exact test + authorities.

elements.name: Power used for a different purpose
elements.layPrompt: Does it seem the real reason for the decision was something other than what the power is for?

grounds.name: Inflexible policy / acting under dictation
grounds.plainName: They followed a rule or an order instead of deciding your case
grounds.oneLine: A discretion was applied so rigidly, or so much at someone else's direction, that your case wasn't really considered.
grounds.whatItMeans: A decision-maker who has a discretion must actually use it. Applying a policy so rigidly that they do not consider your particular circumstances, or simply doing what another person or body directed, can be unlawful.
grounds.plainExample: An application is refused 'because that is our policy', without anyone looking at your specific situation.
whatRelates: You were told 'that is just our policy', with no look at your circumstances.
The decision-maker seemed to be following someone else's direction.
Your particular situation does not seem to have been considered at all.
grounds.whatItIsNot: It does not mean policies are not allowed — only that they cannot be applied with no room for your case.
grounds.test: Fettering a discretion by an inflexible policy, or acting under another's dictation, may make a decision invalid — VERIFY the exact test + authorities.

elements.name: Inflexible application of policy
elements.layPrompt: Was a policy applied without looking at your circumstances?

elements.name: Acting under dictation
elements.layPrompt: Did the decision-maker just do what someone else told them to?

grounds.name: Jurisdictional error
grounds.plainName: The decision-maker went beyond their power
grounds.oneLine: The decision-maker acted outside their power, or asked the wrong question.
grounds.whatItMeans: A decision can be unlawful if the decision-maker acted beyond the power the law gives them — for example by asking the wrong question, ignoring a step the law required first, or misunderstanding the limits of their role. This is a central idea in judicial review, especially for Commonwealth decisions.
grounds.plainExample: A body decides something only a different body had the power to decide.
whatRelates: The decision-maker may not have had the power to make this decision.
A step the law required first seems to have been skipped.
The decision-maker seems to have misunderstood what they were allowed to decide.
grounds.whatItIsNot: It does not mean any mistake — it has to be an error that goes to the decision-maker's power.
grounds.test: An error going to the existence or exercise of power (a jurisdictional error) may make a decision invalid — VERIFY the exact test + authorities.

elements.name: Acting beyond power
elements.layPrompt: Did the decision-maker act outside what the law lets them do?

elements.name: Asking the wrong question
elements.layPrompt: Did they decide on the wrong question, or ignore a required step?

grounds.name: No evidence / mistake of material fact
grounds.plainName: A key finding had no evidence behind it
grounds.oneLine: A finding that mattered was made with no evidence to support it.
grounds.whatItMeans: A decision can be challenged if a finding on a key fact was made with no evidence at all to support it, or rested on a clear mistake about a fact the decision turned on. It is not enough that the evidence was weak — there must be no evidence, or a clear error on a central point.
grounds.plainExample: A debt is raised on the basis you were paid on dates that the records show you were not.
whatRelates: A key finding was made with nothing to support it.
The decision turned on a fact that is simply wrong.
Evidence you gave that contradicts a central finding appears to have been overlooked.
grounds.whatItIsNot: It does not cover a decision you just think got the facts wrong on balance — the error has to be on a critical point with no support.
grounds.test: A material finding made with no evidence, or a decision based on a mistake of a material fact, may be reviewable — VERIFY the exact test + authorities.

elements.name: No evidence for a key finding
elements.layPrompt: Was there an important finding with no evidence at all behind it?

elements.name: Mistake about a central fact
elements.layPrompt: Did the decision rely on a fact that is plainly wrong?

grounds.name: Procedural fairness (natural justice)
grounds.plainName: You weren't given a fair chance
grounds.oneLine: A fair hearing from a decision-maker who doesn't have a closed or biased mind.
grounds.whatItMeans: Before a decision that affects you, the decision-maker usually has to tell you what is being considered and give you a real chance to respond. They also have to be free of bias — they should not have a personal interest in the outcome or have made up their mind in advance.
grounds.plainExample: A payment is cancelled because of a report you were never shown and never had a chance to answer.
whatRelates: You were not told what the decision would be about before it was made.
You were not given a real chance to explain your side.
Information was used against you that you never got to see or respond to.
The decision-maker seemed to have decided before hearing from you, or had a personal stake.
grounds.whatItIsNot: It does not mean you were entitled to win — only that you were entitled to a fair process.
grounds.test: A decision-maker must give a person a fair hearing and be free of bias — VERIFY the exact test + authorities against the corpus.

elements.name: Fair hearing
elements.layPrompt: Were you told what was being decided and given a real chance to respond before the decision was made?

elements.name: No bias
elements.layPrompt: Was there anything suggesting the decision-maker had already made up their mind, or had a personal interest?

grounds.name: Relevant and irrelevant considerations
grounds.plainName: They ignored something they had to weigh, or relied on something they shouldn't
grounds.oneLine: A decision-maker must weigh what the law says matters — and ignore what it says doesn't.
grounds.whatItMeans: A decision-maker must take into account the things the law requires them to consider, and must not rely on things the law says are irrelevant. Leaving out a required consideration, or relying on a forbidden one, can make a decision unlawful.
grounds.plainExample: A housing decision ignores the medical evidence that the rules say must be taken into account.
whatRelates: Something the rules say must be considered appears to have been left out.
The decision relied on something that should not have mattered.
You gave important information that the decision-maker did not appear to weigh.
grounds.whatItIsNot: It does not mean every point you raised had to be accepted — only that the required matters had to be genuinely considered.
grounds.test: A decision-maker must consider the matters the law requires, and must not rely on matters the law forbids — VERIFY the exact test + authorities against the corpus.

elements.name: Ignored a required consideration
elements.layPrompt: Was there something important the law required them to take into account that they seem to have ignored?

elements.name: Relied on an irrelevant consideration
elements.layPrompt: Did they rely on something that shouldn't have mattered to this decision?

grounds.name: Legal unreasonableness
grounds.plainName: The decision was one no reasonable decision-maker could make
grounds.oneLine: Even with power, a decision can be unlawful if it lacks any sensible justification.
grounds.whatItMeans: Even where a decision-maker has the power, the decision can be unlawful if it lacks an evident and intelligible justification on the material before them, or is so unreasonable that no reasonable decision-maker could have reached it. This is a high bar — it is not enough to disagree.
grounds.plainExample: A penalty far out of proportion to a tiny, first-time mistake, with no explanation.
whatRelates: The outcome seems to make no sense on the facts the decision-maker had.
The result is far harsher than the situation called for.
There is no intelligible reason given for the decision.
grounds.whatItIsNot: It does not mean you simply disagree with the decision — it has to lack a sensible basis.
grounds.test: A decision may be unlawful if it lacks an evident and intelligible justification, or is one no reasonable decision-maker could have reached — VERIFY the exact test + authorities.

elements.name: No intelligible justification
elements.layPrompt: Does the outcome seem to make no sense on the facts the decision-maker had?

elements.name: Disproportionate or arbitrary
elements.layPrompt: Was the result far harsher than the situation called for, or seemingly arbitrary?

triage.mrVsJr: Merits review asks 'what is the correct or preferable decision?' — a tribunal stands in the decision-maker's shoes and can change the outcome. Judicial review asks 'was the decision lawful?' — a court checks the process and can set a decision aside, but cannot substitute its own. Choose by what the person wants: a different outcome points to merits review (if available); an unlawful process, or no merits-review right, points to judicial review. VERIFY the avenues for each decision against the data layer.

remedies.mr: affirm, vary, or set aside and substitute the decision (merits review)
remedies.jr: quash / set aside and send back to be remade; cannot substitute a new decision (judicial review)
